message:
feat(core): add Connection::close_without_checkpoint{,_in_place}

Adds two new public close paths on Connection that skip the close-time
WAL checkpoint:

  * `close_without_checkpoint(mut self) -> Result<()>` — consume-self
    variant, mirrors the existing `close()` API.
  * `close_without_checkpoint_in_place(&mut self) -> Result<()>` —
    in-place variant, mirrors the existing `close_in_place(&mut self)`
    API for callers that want to retain the handle on error.

Both route into a refactored internal:

    fn close_internal(
        &mut self,
        best_effort: bool,
        checkpoint_on_close: bool,
    ) -> Result<()>

The existing checkpoint-on-close logic (WAL + writable + no active
concurrent txns) is now gated on `checkpoint_on_close` as well. The
existing entry points preserve their prior semantics:

  * `close_in_place`            -> close_internal(false, true)   (checkpoint)
  * `close_best_effort_in_place`-> close_internal(true,  true)   (checkpoint)
  * `close_without_checkpoint_in_place` -> close_internal(false, false)

Why skip the checkpoint?

When the caller is about to exit the process, or has a read-only or
otherwise-known-clean handle, the passive checkpoint that `close()`
normally performs in WAL mode is pure latency tax — the next opener
will recover and publish the WAL contents anyway. Indexing workloads
in particular (e.g. CASS's lexical rebuild tail) measurably pay for
this on every connection close. Skipping it is safe because:

  * All committed writes remain durably in the WAL; recovery on next
    open publishes them.
  * Transaction state is still finalized (commit_undo on a dirty
    table_lock, plus the usual runtime teardown).
  * Only the opportunistic passive checkpoint is skipped.

Memory databases and read-only connections are unaffected in either
direction because they never checkpoint on close.
